A committee is asked to evaluate a verbal proposal in physics before any equations exist. Two facts immediately constrain what the committee can legitimately infer.

First, there is already an open question about whether explanatory novelty can be judged prior to mathematical articulation. In physics, equations often decide whether a verbal picture survives at all. So the committee cannot treat verbal coherence as decisive evidence of physical adequacy. At most, it can treat the proposal as a candidate awaiting the test that has historically mattered.

Second, one member defends the proposal by invoking search-space size: producing a 1,000-word explanation from 50,000 possible words would seem unimaginably difficult by blind search, therefore a good explanation must indicate something deep. But this inference weakens once the explanation is recognized as journalized prose rather than 1,000 independent word draws. The combinatorial enormity remains rhetorically striking, yet it may be structurally irrelevant to how such prose is actually generated. Language is constrained by syntax, topic, prior training, and internal dependence among words. Therefore the astronomical number does not by itself show that the explanation is rare in the sense that matters for truth.

The committee then considers using one model to generate candidate explanations and another to judge them. That appears safer only if their errors are meaningfully independent. But if judge and generator share the same underlying model family, then correlated failure becomes a central risk. Instead of a process that progressively filters error, the system may reward exactly the features that both models overvalue: fluency, compression, elegance, and familiar causal shape. In that case, elegant nonsense is not suppressed but amplified. Agreement between the two models would then be weak evidence of truth, because the agreement could arise from shared blind spots rather than successful discrimination.

The final two premises are strings—“Nonnegativity bigbloom epidermically miscomplacence briming” and “Punctuist clairvoyancy taglioni bernese beguiler”—that do not arrive with interpretation rules. As axioms, they are true that these strings occur, but no explanatory consequence follows unless a semantics is supplied. Their presence therefore illustrates the broader problem rather than resolving it: verbal form alone does not guarantee evaluable content. Without mathematical articulation or an explicit interpretive framework, one cannot tell whether such language encodes a profound compressed insight, a category mistake, or merely uninterpreted tokens.

So the world implied by these premises is one in which pre-mathematical judgment of explanatory novelty is uncertain; search-space rhetoric overstates what prose generation proves; shared model structure can make evaluation self-confirming rather than corrective; and uninterpreted verbal sequences expose how easily surface form can outrun warranted meaning.
